cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question porte sur un actif financier de type dette relevant d’IFRS 9, et non sur un instrument de capitaux propres.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les faits fournis ne permettent pas d’identifier le modèle économique ; la réponse vise donc d’abord le respect du critère SPPI, puis les bases de classement qui restent ouvertes selon le modèle économique.\\\",\\n    \\\"Le terme contractuel envisagé peut modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux, y compris via un événement déclencheur contractuel.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut respecter SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent uniquement des remboursements de principal et d’intérêts d’un prêt basique et ne diffèrent pas de façon significative d’un instrument sans cette clause. Sinon, l’actif échoue au test SPPI et relève du FVTPL.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause contractuelle modifiant les flux ne fait pas perdre le caractère SPPI : il faut apprécier les flux avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité de survenance du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêts d’un prêt basique et que le modèle économique est de détenir pour encaisser, le classement au coût amorti reste ouvert (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10A, B4.1.18).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels avant et après l’événement déclencheur restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause contingente ne crée pas d’écart significatif de flux par rapport à un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"La caractéristique n’introduit ni levier ni exposition à des risques incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est de détenir l’actif pour encaisser les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’actif peut être mesuré ultérieurement au coût amorti ; sinon cette base est exclue.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: (a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) **the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether **the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, **irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding **if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A contractual cash flow characteristic does not affect the classification of the financial asset if it could have only a **de minimis effect** on the contractual cash flows of the financial asset. To make this determination, an entity must consider the possible effect of the contractual cash flow characteristic in each reporting period and cumulatively over the life of the financial instrument. In addition, if a contractual cash flow characteristic could have an effect on the contractual cash flows that is more than de minimis (either in a single reporting period or cumulatively) but that cash flow characteristic is **not genuine**, it does not affect the classification of a financial asset.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvoci_debt\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par OCI - titres de dette\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le classement en FVOCI pour un instrument de dette reste possible si la clause de variabilité ou de contingence respecte SPPI selon la même analyse que ci-dessus : les flux potentiels doivent demeurer ceux d’un prêt basique (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). En plus, le modèle économique doit être atteint à la fois par l’encaissement des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels avant et après l’événement déclencheur restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause contingente ne crée pas d’écart significatif de flux par rapport à un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"La caractéristique n’introduit ni levier ni exposition à des risques incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est atteint à la fois par l’encaissement des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs financiers.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, le classement en FVOCI pour dette reste disponible ; sinon il ne l’est pas.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: (a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) **the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether **the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, **irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding **if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature**.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvtpl\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause qui modifie les flux introduit une exposition incompatible avec un prêt basique, par exemple un lien avec des prix d’actions, de matières premières, un levier, ou des flux significativement différents d’un instrument sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.9, B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors que l’actif est mesuré au FVTPL dès lors qu’il n’est ni au coût amorti ni en FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause modifiant les flux fait échouer le test SPPI, notamment en créant une exposition à des risques ou une volatilité étrangers à un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou les flux contractuels possibles deviennent significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans la clause contingente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si la clause fait échouer SPPI, le classement résiduel est le FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, contractual terms that introduce **exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest** on the principal amount outstanding.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Leverage** is a contractual cash flow characteristic of some financial assets. Leverage increases the variability of the contractual cash flows with the result that they do not have the economic characteristics of interest. Stand‑alone option, forward and swap contracts are examples of financial assets that include such leverage. Thus, such contracts **do not meet** the condition in paragraphs 4.1.2(b) and 4.1.2A(b) and **cannot be subsequently measured at amortised cost or fair value through other comprehensive income**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding **if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841639971}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Un instrument dont les termes contractuels peuvent modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, notamment en cas de survenance d’un événement déclencheur, peut-il satisfaire au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_10-39-08_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question porte sur un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9, et non sur un passif financier ou un instrument de capitaux propres.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les faits fournis n’indiquent pas la nature précise du déclencheur ni l’ampleur potentielle des variations de flux; l’analyse doit donc répondre au niveau de principe IFRS.\\\",\\n    \\\"La question vise uniquement le critère SPPI (« prêt basique ») et non, à elle seule, la détermination définitive de la catégorie de classement sans information sur le business model.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut satisfaire au critère SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent uniquement des remboursements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’un prêt basique. Sinon, l’actif doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la seule présence d’une clause pouvant modifier le montant ou l’échéancier des flux n’exclut pas automatiquement SPPI; il faut apprécier les flux avant et après le changement et la nature du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si, malgré cette clause, les flux demeurent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt et que l’actif est détenu dans un modèle de collecte, le coût amorti reste possible (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels possibles avant et après le changement restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts basiques du prêt, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif doit être détenu dans un modèle dont l’objectif est de collecter les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le classement au coût amorti est possible seulement si le test SPPI est satisfait malgré la clause contingente et si le business model est « hold to collect ».\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: (a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise **both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows**, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring. The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature. In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_oci\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par OCI\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, l’actif peut aussi satisfaire SPPI malgré une clause de modification des flux, selon la même analyse des scénarios contractuels et du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Si SPPI est respecté et que le modèle économique combine collecte des flux et cession, la catégorie FVOCI devient applicable (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels possibles restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts basiques du prêt, l’effet sur les flux ne doit pas être significatif par rapport à un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif doit être détenu dans un modèle économique de collecte et de vente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le classement FVOCI est possible si la clause n’empêche pas SPPI et si le business model est « collect and sell ».\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: (a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by **both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets** and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise **both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows**, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring. The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature. In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JV résultat s’impose si la clause expose l’instrument à des flux incompatibles avec un prêt basique, par exemple si les scénarios contractuels ne restent pas limités à principal et intérêt ou si l’effet du déclencheur non lié aux risques basiques est significatif (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors que tout actif ne remplissant ni 4.1.2 ni 4.1.2A est mesuré à la juste valeur par résultat (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause fait échouer le test SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien les flux potentiels diffèrent significativement de ceux d’un instrument identique sans la clause contingente.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien la clause introduit une exposition à des risques ou volatilités non cohérents avec un prêt basique.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI échoue à cause de la clause de modification des flux, l’actif doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost** in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 **or at fair value through other comprehensive income** in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is **unrelated to a basic lending arrangement**, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, **do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature. In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841670028}

{"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’il s’agit d’un actif financier entrant dans le champ d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question vise uniquement l’effet de clauses contractuelles modifiant l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie sur le test SPPI, sans autres faits spécifiques sur le modèle économique de gestion.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’aucune désignation volontaire à la juste valeur par le résultat pour éliminer une incohérence comptable n’est analysée ici au-delà de ce qui est déjà prévu par les approches identifiées.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut encore satisfaire le critère SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt compatibles avec un prêt basique et ne diffèrent pas significativement lorsqu’un déclencheur non lié aux risques de base existe (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). La catégorie de mesure dépend ensuite aussi du modèle économique (IFRS 9 4.1.2, 4.1.2A).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti peut s’appliquer si la clause modifiant les flux n’empêche pas le test SPPI: il faut analyser les flux avant et après la modification, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels, les flux restent du principal et de l’intérêt d’un prêt basique, l’actif peut relever du coût amorti, mais seulement si le modèle économique est de détenir pour encaisser les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, avant et après modification, restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts de base du prêt, les flux contractuels ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est de détenir les actifs pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, la présence d’un déclencheur n’exclut pas en soi la mesure au coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_oci\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, l’instrument peut aussi rester dans une catégorie fondée sur SPPI malgré une clause de modification des flux, pour les mêmes raisons d’analyse des flux contractuels avant et après le déclenchement (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Toutefois, cette approche ne s’applique que si le modèle économique consiste à la fois à encaisser les flux contractuels et à vendre les actifs financiers (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, avant et après modification, restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts de base du prêt, les flux contractuels ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs financiers.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"La clause contingente n’empêche pas à elle seule le FVOCI si le test SPPI reste satisfait et si le modèle économique mixte est démontré.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_profit_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par le résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la juste valeur par le résultat s’applique si la clause contractuelle introduit une exposition incompatible avec un prêt basique, par exemple à des prix d’actions, de matières premières ou un effet de levier, ou si les flux modifiés ne sont pas SPPI (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.8A, B4.1.9, B4.1.14). Elle est aussi la catégorie résiduelle lorsqu’aucune des catégories SPPI ne s’applique (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause modifiant les flux expose l’instrument à des risques ou à une volatilité non liés à un prêt basique, ou les flux contractuels ne satisfont pas le test SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien l’actif n’est pas mesuré au coût amorti ni au FVOCI au regard d’IFRS 9.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le déclencheur ou la formule de flux fait échouer le test SPPI, l’instrument bascule en juste valeur par le résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). However, **contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.8A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In assessing whether the contractual cash flows of a financial asset are consistent with a basic lending arrangement, an entity may have to consider the different elements of interest separately. The assessment of interest focuses on what an entity is being compensated for, rather than how much compensation an entity receives. Nonetheless, the amount of compensation an entity receives may indicate that the entity is being compensated for something other than basic lending risks and costs. **Contractual cash flows are inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement if they are indexed to a variable that is not a basic lending risk or cost (for example, the value of equity instruments or the price of a commodity) or if they represent a share of the debtor’s revenue or profit,** even if such contractual terms are common in the market in which the entity operates.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Leverage is a contractual cash flow characteristic of some financial assets. **Leverage increases the variability of the contractual cash flows with the result that they do not have the economic characteristics of interest.** Stand‑alone option, forward and swap contracts are examples of financial assets that include such leverage. **Thus, such contracts do not meet the condition in paragraphs 4.1.2(b) and 4.1.2A(b) and cannot be subsequently measured at amortised cost or fair value through other comprehensive income.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. This list of examples is not exhaustive.\\\\nInstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety.\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer.\\\\nInstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates). **The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nInstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest. **The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nIf interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nThe fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.)\\\\nInstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod. **The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. **The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841681424}

L’instrument respecte le critère seulement si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt compatibles avec un prêt basique; sinon, il échoue au SPPI et relève du FVTPL.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause contingente ne fait pas sortir l’instrument du cadre d’un prêt basique et si les flux pouvant survenir avant et après le déclencheur restent uniquement principal et intérêts (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Même si le SPPI est respecté, cette catégorie ne s’applique que si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique visant à collecter les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument sans la clause contingente lorsque le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts de prêt basiques.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause n’introduit pas d’exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique visant à collecter les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_oci\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par OCI\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVOCI n’est envisageable que si la clause contingente passe le test SPPI selon la même analyse des flux avant et après déclenchement (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). En plus, l’actif doit être détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est à la fois d’encaisser les flux contractuels et de vendre les actifs financiers (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_profit_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause contingente expose le porteur à des risques ou à une volatilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique, de sorte que les flux ne sont pas SPPI (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.14). IFRS 9 prévoit alors que l’actif est mesuré à la juste valeur par résultat dès lors qu’il ne remplit pas les conditions du coût amorti ou de la JVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause introduit une exposition à des variables non liées aux risques et coûts de prêt basiques, ou produit des flux significativement différents incompatibles avec SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif ne remplit pas les conditions du coût amorti ou de la JVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le SPPI échoue en raison de la clause, la classification résiduelle est le FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. This list of examples is not exhaustive.\\\\nInstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety.\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer.\\\\nInstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates).**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nInstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest.**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nIf interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nThe fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.)\\\\nInstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod.**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841785455}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Un instrument dont les termes contractuels peuvent modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, notamment en cas de survenance d’un événement déclencheur, peut-il satisfaire au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_10-39-08_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question porte uniquement sur le critère SPPI d’IFRS 9 pour un actif financier, et non sur la détermination du modèle économique.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait supplémentaire n’est fourni sur la nature exacte de l’événement déclencheur, l’ampleur de la modification des flux ou l’existence d’une indexation à des variables non compatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un instrument dont les flux peuvent changer peut encore satisfaire au critère SPPI si, sur toute la durée de vie et dans les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt compatibles avec un prêt basique (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Si le déclencheur ou la formule introduit une exposition à des risques non basiques ou des écarts significatifs, le critère SPPI échoue.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le simple fait qu’un terme contractuel modifie l’échéancier ou le montant des flux n’exclut pas SPPI: IFRS 9 exige d’analyser les flux avant et après la modification, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Le coût amorti n’est possible que si, en plus de ce test SPPI, l’actif est détenu dans un modèle visant à collecter les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2). Si le déclencheur crée des flux significativement différents d’un prêt basique, l’approche ne s’applique pas (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A, B4.1.14).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels possibles, avant et après déclenchement, doivent rester des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts de prêt basiques, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif doit être détenu dans un modèle économique de conservation pour encaissement.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le classement au coût amorti reste possible, mais seulement après validation du test SPPI sur tous les scénarios contractuels et du modèle économique hold to collect.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. La FVOCI est donc possible si les flux demeurent SPPI malgré la clause et si le modèle économique combine encaissement des flux et ventes (IFRS 9 4.1.2A). a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n
