cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’instrument est un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9 et que seule la question du critère SPPI est en cause; le test du modèle économique n’est pas déterminable à partir des faits fournis.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un terme contractuel déclencheur n’exclut pas automatiquement le critère SPPI. L’instrument peut rester compatible avec un « prêt basique » si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt, sans exposition significative à des risques non basiques.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si le terme déclencheur respecte le test SPPI et si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique de collecte des flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2, IFRS 9 B4.1.10). IFRS 9 précise qu’un changement possible du montant ou de l’échéancier des flux n’exclut pas le SPPI si les flux avant et après changement restent du principal et de l’intérêt; si le déclencheur ne porte pas directement sur des risques de prêt basiques, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique doit être de détention pour encaisser les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuels, le terme déclencheur ne doit pas créer des flux significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause lorsqu’il ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts basiques de prêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le terme ne doit pas introduire d’exposition à l’equity, aux matières premières, à l’effet de levier ou à un autre rendement incompatible avec un prêt basique.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’actif peut être mesuré au coût amorti; sinon, ce traitement est exclu.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvoci\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la FVOCI peut s’appliquer si le terme déclencheur passe le test SPPI selon la même analyse que ci-dessus et si le modèle économique combine encaissement des flux et cession (IFRS 9 4.1.2A, IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Le simple fait qu’un événement puisse modifier les flux n’est donc pas rédhibitoire; ce qui compte est que les flux contractuels demeurent compatibles avec un prêt basique, y compris en présence d’un déclencheur non directement lié aux risques basiques, à condition que l’effet ne soit pas significatif (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique doit être de collecte des flux contractuels et de vente.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le terme déclencheur doit laisser subsister des flux SPPI dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts basiques de prêt, son effet sur les flux ne doit pas être significativement différent de celui d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le SPPI est satisfait et que le modèle économique est collecter et vendre, la FVOCI peut être retenue.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvtpl\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la FVPL s’impose si le terme déclencheur fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple parce qu’il introduit une exposition à des variables non basiques, à l’effet de levier, ou à des flux significativement différents d’un prêt basique (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, IFRS 9 B4.1.8A, IFRS 9 B4.1.9, IFRS 9 B4.1.10A). Autrement dit, l’existence du déclencheur n’emporte pas automatiquement la FVPL, mais elle y conduit dès que les flux cessent d’être uniquement du principal et de l’intérêt au sens d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le terme déclencheur expose l’investisseur à des risques ou à une volatilité sans lien avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le terme introduit un levier ou une indexation à l’equity, aux matières premières, à un indice carbone de marché ou à une autre variable non basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels dans les scénarios possibles deviennent significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans la clause contingente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le SPPI n’est pas respecté à cause de la clause déclencheuse, l’actif doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). However, **contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.8A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In assessing whether the contractual cash flows of a financial asset are consistent with a basic lending arrangement, an entity may have to consider the different elements of interest separately. The assessment of interest focuses on what an entity is being compensated for, rather than how much compensation an entity receives. Nonetheless, the amount of compensation an entity receives may indicate that the entity is being compensated for something other than basic lending risks and costs. **Contractual cash flows are inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement if they are indexed to a variable that is not a basic lending risk or cost (for example, the value of equity instruments or the price of a commodity) or if they represent a share of the debtor’s revenue or profit, even if such contractual terms are common in the market in which the entity operates.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Leverage is a contractual cash flow characteristic of some financial assets. **Leverage increases the variability of the contractual cash flows with the result that they do not have the economic characteristics of interest.** Stand‑alone option, forward and swap contracts are examples of financial assets that include such leverage. **Thus, such contracts do not meet the condition in paragraphs 4.1.2(b) and 4.1.2A(b) and cannot be subsequently measured at amortised cost or fair value through other comprehensive income.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841885793}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Un instrument peut comporter des dispositions contractuelles entraînant une modification de l’échéancier ou du montant des flux de trésorerie, par exemple à la survenance d’un événement déclencheur. Dans ce cas, peut-il encore répondre au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_10-39-08_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question porte uniquement sur l’appréciation du critère SPPI d’un actif financier au regard d’une clause contractuelle modifiant l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie en cas d’événement déclencheur.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucune information n’est fournie sur le modèle économique de détention de l’actif; l’analyse des catégories de mesure est donc conditionnelle à ce point.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’instrument est un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut encore satisfaire au critère SPPI si, malgré la clause contingente, les flux contractuels restent compatibles avec un prêt basique dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles et ne sont pas significativement différents d’un instrument comparable sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non liés à un prêt basique, le test SPPI échoue et l’actif relève alors du FVTPL (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, 4.1.4).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause déclenchée ne fait pas perdre le caractère SPPI: il faut apprécier les flux pouvant survenir avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité de survenance (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, les flux demeurent ceux d’un prêt basique et que l’actif est détenu pour encaisser les flux contractuels, la mesure au coût amorti reste possible (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû, avant et après l’événement déclencheur.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, la clause ne conduit pas à des flux significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique visant à encaisser les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, la présence d’une clause contingente n’empêche pas, à elle seule, la mesure au coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding **if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvoci\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la clause contingente n’exclut pas automatiquement le FVOCI si le test SPPI reste satisfait selon l’analyse des flux avant et après modification (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). En revanche, cette catégorie n’est applicable que si, en plus, l’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est à la fois d’encaisser les flux contractuels et de vendre les actifs financiers (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels restent SPPI malgré la clause déclenchée.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, l’écart de flux avec un instrument comparable sans la clause n’est pas significatif.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique visant à la fois l’encaissement des flux contractuels et la vente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI est respecté et que le modèle économique est « collecter et vendre », la présence du déclencheur n’empêche pas le classement en FVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding **if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvtpl\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique, de sorte que les flux ne sont plus uniquement principal et intérêts (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.14). Il s’applique aussi par défaut si les conditions du coût amorti ou du FVOCI ne sont pas remplies, ou sur désignation initiale pour éliminer un décalage comptable (IFRS 9 4.1.4, 4.1.5).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause fait échouer le test SPPI en introduisant des flux incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien les conditions de modèle économique du coût amorti ou du FVOCI ne sont pas remplies.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien l’actif est irrévocablement désigné à la juste valeur par résultat à l’origine pour réduire un mismatch comptable.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si la clause contingente fait perdre le caractère SPPI, l’actif ne peut pas être mesuré au coût amorti ni en FVOCI et bascule en FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A–B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. This list of examples is not exhaustive. **InstrumentF Instrument F is a bond that is convertible into a fixed number of equity instruments of the issuer.** The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety. **The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer. ... **InstrumentI Instrument I is a loan with an interest rate that is adjusted every reporting period to track the movements in a market-determined carbon price index during the preceding reporting period. The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** The contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5–5.7.6).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.5\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Despite paragraphs 4.1.1–4.1.4, **an entity may, at initial recognition, irrevocably designate a financial asset as measured at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency** (sometimes referred to as an ‘accounting mismatch’) that would otherwise arise from measuring assets or liabilities or recognising the gains and losses on them on different bases (see paragraphs B4.1.29–B4.1.32).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841896434}

Dans ce cas, peut-il encore répondre au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_10-39-08_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’instrument en cause est un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question porte uniquement sur l’effet d’une clause contractuelle modifiant les flux à la survenance d’un événement déclencheur, sans autres caractéristiques non décrites.\\\",\\n    \\\"Le modèle économique de détention de l’actif n’est pas précisé ; la conclusion sur le classement entre coût amorti et FVOCI dépendra donc de ce point en plus du test SPPI.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut encore satisfaire le critère SPPI si, malgré la clause déclenchante, les flux contractuels possibles restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt et ne diffèrent pas significativement d’un prêt basique comparable (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Si la clause introduit une exposition non compatible avec un prêt basique, l’actif échoue au test SPPI et relève du FVTPL (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, 4.1.4).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti reste possible si la clause qui modifie l’échéancier ou le montant des flux ne fait pas échouer le test SPPI : il faut apprécier les flux pouvant survenir avant et après le déclenchement, sans tenir compte de la probabilité de l’événement (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). En plus, l’actif ne peut être au coût amorti que s’il est détenu dans un modèle visant à collecter les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause déclenchante ne doit pas introduire d’exposition à des risques ou une variabilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux doivent rester des paiements de principal et d’intérêt, ou ne pas être significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause lorsque IFRS 9 B4.1.10A s’applique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique doit être de type « hold to collect ».\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’actif peut être classé au coût amorti ; sinon, ce traitement ne s’applique pas.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvoci_debt\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par OCI (titres de dette)\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la FVOCI demeure possible si la clause déclenchante est compatible avec SPPI selon la même analyse des flux avant et après l’événement déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Toutefois, ce classement n’est applicable que si le modèle économique combine encaissement des flux contractuels et cession des actifs (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause déclenchante doit laisser subsister des flux contractuels de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts de base d’un prêt, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique doit être de type « collect and sell ».\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si SPPI est satisfait et que le modèle économique est mixte collecte/vente, l’actif peut être classé en FVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fvtpl\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause déclenchante fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple parce qu’elle introduit une exposition à des risques non propres à un prêt basique ou une divergence significative des flux (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors que l’actif est mesuré à la juste valeur par résultat s’il ne relève ni du coût amorti ni de la FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause déclenchante introduit une exposition incompatible avec un prêt basique, ou les flux possibles deviennent significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument comparable sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif ne remplit donc pas la condition SPPI nécessaire au coût amorti ou à la FVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI échoue à cause de la clause contractuelle, le classement résiduel est le FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5–5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A–B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778841982901}

{"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’il s’agit d’un actif financier entrant dans le champ d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la seule question posée est l’effet de termes contractuels pouvant modifier le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie sur le critère SPPI, sans autres caractéristiques contractuelles supplémentaires non décrites.\\\",\\n    \\\"Le modèle économique de détention n’est pas précisé ; en conséquence, les bases de mesure à retenir dépendent, si le test SPPI est satisfait, du modèle économique applicable.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut respecter le critère SPPI, mais seulement si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt compatibles avec un prêt basique. Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non basiques ou des écarts significatifs de flux, le test SPPI échoue.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la présence d’une clause pouvant modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux n’exclut pas, à elle seule, le SPPI ; IFRS 9 exige d’analyser les flux pouvant survenir avant et après le changement contractuel (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si ces flux demeurent uniquement des remboursements de principal et d’intérêt et si l’actif est détenu pour encaisser les flux contractuels, la mesure au coût amorti reste possible (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, avant et après l’événement déclencheur, restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause ne crée pas d’exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est de détenir les actifs pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si la clause contingente reste compatible avec un prêt basique et que le modèle est hold to collect, l’actif peut être comptabilisé au coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le fait qu’une clause déclencheuse modifie les flux n’empêche pas nécessairement le SPPI, à condition que les flux restent compatibles avec un prêt basique sur toute la vie de l’instrument (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si ce test est satisfait et que le modèle économique consiste à la fois à encaisser les flux et à vendre les actifs, la mesure à la JVOCI est permise (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels potentiels demeurent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles pertinents.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause ne rend pas les flux significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette caractéristique, lorsque cette analyse est requise.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le SPPI est satisfait malgré la clause de variation et que le modèle est collect and sell, l’actif peut être classé en JVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a) the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b) the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1–B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVPL s’applique si la clause modifiant les flux fait échouer le SPPI, par exemple en introduisant une exposition à des risques non liés à un prêt basique, ou si l’actif ne relève pas des catégories coût amorti/JVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4, B4.1.7A). Elle peut aussi résulter d’une désignation irrévocable initiale pour éliminer ou réduire fortement un décalage comptable (IFRS 9 4.1.5).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause entraîne des flux incompatibles avec un prêt basique, notamment une exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité non basiques.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou l’entité désigne irrévocablement l’actif à la JVPL dès l’origine pour éliminer ou réduire significativement un décalage comptable.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le SPPI échoue en raison de la clause contingente, ou en cas de désignation pour mismatch comptable, l’actif est mesuré à la JVPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.5\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Despite paragraphs 4.1.1–4.1.4, **an entity may, at initial recognition, irrevocably designate a financial asset as measured at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency** (sometimes referred to as an ‘accounting mismatch’) that would otherwise arise from measuring assets or liabilities or recognising the gains and losses on them on different bases.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks and costs associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850095487}
