cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question vise un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9, et non un instrument de capitaux propres.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait complémentaire n’est donné sur la nature précise du déclencheur, l’ampleur de la variation des flux ou le business model ; l’analyse porte donc sur la possibilité de satisfaire au test SPPI en présence d’une clause modifiant les flux.\\\",\\n    \\\"L’instrument concerné est analysé sur la base de ses seuls termes contractuels.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut satisfaire au critère SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux demeurent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt compatibles avec un prêt basique et ne diffèrent pas significativement d’un instrument sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Sinon, il échoue au test SPPI et relève de la juste valeur par résultat (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause modifiant l’échéancier ou le montant des flux ne fait pas sortir l’instrument du cadre SPPI ; IFRS 9 impose d’examiner les flux avant et après la modification, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). En plus, si le déclencheur ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, les flux doivent, dans tous les scénarios contractuels, ne pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A). Le classement au coût amorti suppose aussi le business model « hold to collect » (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le business model est de détenir l’actif pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, avant et après activation de la clause, restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut rester éligible au coût amorti ; sinon, ce traitement ne s’applique pas.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met:\\\\n(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and\\\\n(b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**.\\\\nParagraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, **irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring**. The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature. In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la présence d’une clause de variation des flux n’exclut pas en soi le SPPI ; il faut appliquer le même examen des flux avant et après modification et, le cas échéant, le test de différence non significative entre scénarios contractuels (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). Si ce test SPPI est satisfait, la JVOCI n’est possible que lorsque le business model combine encaissement des flux contractuels et ventes d’actifs financiers (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le business model est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente d’actifs financiers.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels, avant et après activation de la clause, restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, les flux ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI est respecté et que le business model est « collect and sell », l’instrument peut être classé en JVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met:\\\\n(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by **both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets** and\\\\n(b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**.\\\\nParagraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, **irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring**. The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature. In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVR s’applique si la clause de modification des flux introduit une exposition incompatible avec un prêt basique, par exemple un lien avec des prix d’actions, de matières premières ou une autre variable non liée aux risques et coûts de prêt (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.8A). Elle s’applique aussi si, après l’analyse exigée pour le déclencheur, les flux possibles ne sont pas SPPI ; dans ce cas, l’actif est mesuré à la juste valeur par résultat faute de satisfaire à IFRS 9 4.1.2 ou 4.1.2A (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause de variation expose les flux à des risques ou à une volatilité incompatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou l’analyse des flux avant et après déclenchement montre que le test SPPI n’est pas satisfait.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI échoue, l’instrument doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A**. However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.8A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In assessing whether the contractual cash flows of a financial asset are consistent with a basic lending arrangement, an entity may have to consider the different elements of interest separately. The assessment of interest focuses on what an entity is being compensated for, rather than how much compensation an entity receives. Nonetheless, the amount of compensation an entity receives may indicate that the entity is being compensated for something other than basic lending risks and costs. **Contractual cash flows are inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement if they are indexed to a variable that is not a basic lending risk or cost (for example, the value of equity instruments or the price of a commodity) or if they represent a share of the debtor’s revenue or profit**, even if such contractual terms are common in the market in which the entity operates.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850115137}

{"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu'il s'agit d'un actif financier relevant d'IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question porte sur l'effet d'une clause contractuelle modifiant le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie sur le test SPPI, sans autres caractéristiques contractuelles non décrites.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que le modèle économique de détention de l'actif n'est pas précisé dans les faits et doit donc être traité comme une condition distincte de la conclusion SPPI.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui. Un tel instrument peut respecter le critère SPPI si, en tenant compte de tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d'intérêts, ou ne s'en écartent pas de façon significative lorsque le déclencheur n'est pas lié directement aux risques et coûts d'un prêt basique (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). La catégorie de classement dépend ensuite aussi du modèle économique (IFRS 9 4.1.2, 4.1.2A, 4.1.4).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n'est possible que si la clause de modification des flux n'empêche pas le test SPPI : l'entité doit apprécier les flux avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si, en plus, l'actif est détenu dans un modèle de conservation pour encaisser les flux contractuels, le classement au coût amorti est permis (IFRS 9 4.1.2).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels demeurent uniquement des paiements de principal et d'intérêts dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l'événement déclencheur n'est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d'un prêt basique, les flux avec la clause ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d'un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L'actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l'objectif est de détenir les actifs pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par les autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la présence d'une clause modifiant les flux n'exclut pas en soi SPPI ; il faut tester les flux contractuels potentiels sur toute la durée de vie de l'instrument (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si SPPI est satisfait et que le modèle économique combine encaissement des flux contractuels et vente, la catégorie FVOCI devient applicable (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels demeurent uniquement des paiements de principal et d'intérêts dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l'événement déclencheur n'est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d'un prêt basique, les flux avec la clause ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d'un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L'actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l'objectif est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente d'actifs financiers.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l'instrument peut être mesuré ultérieurement à la FVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JV par résultat s'applique si la clause de modification fait échouer SPPI, par exemple parce que les flux ne sont plus ceux d'un prêt basique ou diffèrent significativement dans un scénario contractuel pertinent (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A, B4.1.14). Elle s'applique aussi par défaut si l'actif ne relève ni du coût amorti ni de la FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le test SPPI échoue en raison de la clause modifiant le calendrier ou le montant des flux.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien l'actif ne satisfait pas aux conditions de modèle économique requises pour le coût amorti ou la FVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si SPPI n'est pas respecté, l'instrument doit être mesuré ultérieurement à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. This list of examples is not exhaustive.\\\\nInstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety.\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer.\\\\nInstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates).**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nInstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest.**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nIf interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nThe fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.)\\\\nInstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod.**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850119817}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question porte sur un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9, et non sur un instrument de capitaux propres.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les faits fournis ne précisent pas le modèle économique de détention; l’analyse vise donc d’abord le critère SPPI, puis ses conséquences de classement selon le modèle économique applicable.\\\",\\n    \\\"L’instrument contient une ou plusieurs clauses contractuelles pouvant modifier le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, y compris via un événement déclencheur.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut satisfaire au critère SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent compatibles avec un prêt basique. Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non basiques ou des flux significativement différents, le test SPPI échoue et l’actif relève du FVTPL.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause de modification des flux n’empêche pas le critère SPPI: l’entité doit apprécier tous les flux pouvant survenir avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité de l’événement déclencheur (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt d’un prêt basique (IFRS 9 4.1.2(b), IFRS 9 B4.1.7A) et si le modèle économique est la collecte des flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2(a)), le traitement au coût amorti s’applique.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est de détenir l’actif pour collecter les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause déclenchée n’introduit pas d’exposition significative à un risque ou à une volatilité non liés à un prêt basique.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’actif reste éligible à une mesure ultérieure au coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with **a basic lending arrangement**. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le classement en FVOCI est possible si la même analyse SPPI est satisfaite malgré la clause de changement de flux (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, IFRS 9 B4.1.10A). Lorsque l’événement déclencheur ne se rapporte pas directement aux risques et coûts basiques, l’actif peut encore être SPPI si les flux, dans tous les scénarios, ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A); il faut aussi que le modèle économique soit à la fois collecte et vente (IFRS 9 4.1.2A(a)).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est atteint à la fois par la collecte des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts basiques, les flux ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’actif peut être mesuré ultérieurement à la FVOCI plutôt qu’au FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. **In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique dès que la clause de modification des flux fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple si elle expose l’actif à des risques non liés à un prêt basique ou à un levier (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, IFRS 9 B4.1.9). C’est aussi l’issue si, pour un déclencheur non directement lié aux risques basiques, les flux deviennent significativement différents dans un scénario contractuel possible (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A); IFRS 9 4.1.4 en fait alors la catégorie résiduelle.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le critère SPPI n’est pas respecté, l’actif doit être classé au FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Leverage is a contractual cash flow characteristic of some financial assets. Leverage increases the variability of the contractual cash flows with the result that they do not have the economic characteristics of interest.** Stand‑alone option, forward and swap contracts are examples of financial assets that include such leverage. **Thus, such contracts do not meet the condition in paragraphs 4.1.2(b) and 4.1.2A(b) and cannot be subsequently measured at amortised cost or fair value through other comprehensive income.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. **In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850198205}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Lorsqu’un instrument comporte des clauses contractuelles susceptibles d’altérer l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, par exemple à la suite d’un événement déclencheur, respecte-t-il le critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_13-00-12_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’il s’agit d’un actif financier entrant dans le champ d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question porte d’abord sur le respect du critère SPPI par la clause contractuelle, sans informations suffisantes sur le modèle économique de détention.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la clause évoquée est une clause contractuelle authentique pouvant modifier le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie sur la durée de vie de l’instrument.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Un instrument avec clause modifiant les flux peut respecter SPPI, mais seulement si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’IFRS 9. Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non compatibles avec un prêt basique ou des écarts significatifs, SPPI échoue et l’actif relève du FVTPL.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause de déclenchement ne fait pas perdre le caractère SPPI et si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle visant à collecter les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2, IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, il faut vérifier que, dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, les flux ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est de détenir l’actif pour en collecter les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Avant et après l’effet de la clause, les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, les flux dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le classement au coût amorti n’est possible qu’après validation du SPPI puis du modèle économique hold to collect.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: (a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVOCI n’est envisageable que si la clause respecte SPPI selon la même analyse des flux avant et après modification (IFRS 9 4.1.2A, IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Il faut en plus que le modèle économique soit de collecter les flux contractuels et de vendre les actifs; à défaut d’information sur ce point, l’approche n’est applicable qu’à cette condition (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est réalisé à la fois par la collecte des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs financiers.\\\",\\n        \\\"Avant et après l’effet de la clause, les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts d’un prêt basique, les flux dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le classement en FVOCI n’est possible que si le test SPPI est satisfait et si le modèle économique est collect-and-sell.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: (a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are **solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple si elle introduit une exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité non liés à un prêt basique, ou si les flux peuvent devenir significativement différents du benchmark (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, IFRS 9 B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors que l’actif est mesuré au FVTPL lorsqu’il ne remplit pas les conditions du coût amorti ou du FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause contractuelle fait échouer le critère SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien les flux contractuels ne remplissent pas les conditions du coût amorti ou du FVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si SPPI n’est pas respecté à cause de la clause, la catégorie résiduelle est la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). However, **contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement**, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, **do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, **in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different** from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850207893}
