cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’instrument en cause est un actif financier relevant d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la seule question à trancher est l’effet des clauses contractuelles modifiant le montant ou l’échéancier des flux de trésorerie sur le critère SPPI, sans autres caractéristiques non décrites.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que le modèle économique de gestion de l’actif n’est pas précisé ; en conséquence, l’analyse distingue d’abord le test SPPI puis la catégorie de mesure qui en découle.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"La présence d’une clause modifiant les flux n’exclut pas automatiquement le critère SPPI. L’instrument respecte le critère seulement si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêts cohérents avec un prêt basique et ne sont pas significativement différents lorsque le déclencheur n’est pas lié aux risques/coûts de base (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A, B4.1.18).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause de déclenchement ne fait pas sortir l’actif du critère SPPI et si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle visant à percevoir les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10). Lorsqu’un terme peut modifier le montant ou l’échéancier, il faut apprécier les flux avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclencheur ; si tous restent compatibles avec un prêt basique, le coût amorti demeure envisageable (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.18).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est de détenir les actifs pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur n’est pas directement lié aux risques et coûts de base d’un prêt, les flux contractuels ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut être classé au coût amorti ; sinon, cette catégorie est exclue.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows** (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**A contractual cash flow characteristic does not affect the classification of the financial asset if it could have only a de minimis effect on the contractual cash flows of the financial asset.** To make this determination, an entity must consider the possible effect of the contractual cash flow characteristic in each reporting period and cumulatively over the life of the financial instrument. In addition, if a contractual cash flow characteristic could have an effect on the contractual cash flows that is more than de minimis (either in a single reporting period or cumulatively) but that cash flow characteristic is not genuine, it does not affect the classification of a financial asset. **A cash flow characteristic is not genuine if it affects the instrument’s contractual cash flows only on the occurrence of an event that is extremely rare, highly abnormal and very unlikely to occur.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVOCI n’est possible que si la clause de déclenchement satisfait au test SPPI et si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle mixte de collecte et de vente (IFRS 9 4.1.2A, B4.1.10). Si le déclencheur n’est pas lié directement aux risques et coûts de base, IFRS 9 impose de vérifier que les flux, dans tous les scénarios contractuels, ne sont pas significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente d’actifs financiers.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû.\\\",\\n        \\\"En présence d’un déclencheur non directement lié aux risques et coûts de base d’un prêt, l’effet de la clause ne rend pas les flux significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut être classé en JVOCI ; sinon, cette catégorie est exclue.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows** (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but **the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs.** For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVR s’applique si la clause de déclenchement fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple si les flux sont exposés à des risques sans lien avec un prêt basique ou deviennent significativement différents des flux d’un instrument simple comparable (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors la JVR comme catégorie résiduelle lorsque l’actif ne peut pas être mesuré au coût amorti ni en JVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les clauses contractuelles modifiant les flux font échouer le test SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien l’actif ne remplit pas les conditions de modèle économique requises pour le coût amorti ou la JVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI échoue à cause de la clause, l’instrument doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but **the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs.** For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850220049}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Certains instruments prévoient des termes contractuels pouvant modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, notamment en cas d’événement déclencheur : un tel instrument peut-il néanmoins être considéré comme respectant le critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_13-00-12_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question vise un actif financier entrant dans le champ de la classification d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"Le modèle économique de détention n’est pas précisé ; l’analyse porte donc d’abord sur le critère SPPI, puis sur la catégorie de mesure qui en découle selon le modèle économique.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait supplémentaire n’indique que la clause déclenchée est, en soi, liée soit à des risques de prêt basiques, soit à des variables étrangères à un prêt basique.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un instrument avec clause modifiant les flux peut encore satisfaire SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent uniquement des remboursements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’un prêt basique. Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non basiques ou des flux significativement différents, le critère SPPI échoue.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, une clause contractuelle pouvant modifier le montant ou l’échéancier des flux n’exclut pas automatiquement SPPI : il faut apprécier les flux avant et après le changement, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclenchement (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si ces flux restent ceux d’un prêt basique et que l’actif est détenu pour encaisser les flux contractuels, la mesure au coût amorti reste possible (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est de percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, la clause déclenchée ne fait pas naître des flux significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause, lorsque l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié à des risques/couts de prêt basiques.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause n’introduit pas d’exposition à des risques ou volatilités étrangers à un prêt basique ; toute incidence éventuelle n’est au plus que de minimis ou non genuine.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut rester éligible au coût amorti malgré la clause de déclenchement.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A contractual cash flow characteristic does not affect the classification of the financial asset **if it could have only a de minimis effect on the contractual cash flows of the financial asset.** To make this determination, an entity must consider the possible effect of the contractual cash flow characteristic in each reporting period and cumulatively over the life of the financial instrument. In addition, if a contractual cash flow characteristic could have an effect on the contractual cash flows that is more than de minimis (either in a single reporting period or cumulatively) but that cash flow characteristic is not genuine, it does not affect the classification of a financial asset. **A cash flow characteristic is not genuine if it affects the instrument’s contractual cash flows only on the occurrence of an event that is extremely rare, highly abnormal and very unlikely to occur.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette même situation, la présence d’un trigger n’empêche pas non plus l’accès à la catégorie FVOCI si le test SPPI reste satisfait après analyse de la clause contingente (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A). La différence avec le coût amorti tient ici uniquement au modèle économique, qui doit combiner encaissement des flux contractuels et ventes (IFRS 9 4.1.2A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est atteint à la fois par l’encaissement des flux contractuels et par la vente d’actifs financiers.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause déclenchée laisse subsister des flux exclusivement constitués de principal et d’intérêt sur le principal restant dû.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si l’événement déclencheur n’est pas directement lié à des risques/couts de prêt basiques, les flux dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles ne doivent pas être significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI est respecté mais que le modèle économique est mixte collecte/vente, la catégorie pertinente devient la FVOCI et non le coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVPR devient le traitement applicable si la clause déclenchée fait échouer SPPI, par exemple parce qu’elle expose l’instrument à des risques ou à une volatilité sans lien avec un prêt basique, tels que des indices actions ou matières premières (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A). Les exemples de conversion en actions, taux inverse ou indexation à un indice carbone de marché illustrent cette issue (IFRS 9 B4.1.14).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause modifiant les flux introduit une exposition à des variables non compatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien, après analyse de tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, les flux deviennent significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans la clause contingente.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si SPPI échoue du fait de la clause contractuelle, l’actif ne peut pas être classé dans une catégorie fondée sur SPPI et relève alors de la JVPR.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.1\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Unless paragraph 4.1.5 applies, **an entity shall classify financial assets as subsequently measured at amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income or fair value through profit or loss** on the basis of both: (a)the entity’s business model for managing the financial assets and (b)the contractual cash flow characteristics of the financial asset.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are **not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** This list of examples is not exhaustive.\\\\nInstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety.\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer.\\\\nInstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates).**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nInstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest.**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nIf interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nThe fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.)\\\\nInstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod.**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850309459}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Certains instruments prévoient des termes contractuels pouvant modifier l’échéancier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie, notamment en cas d’événement déclencheur : un tel instrument peut-il néanmoins être considéré comme respectant le critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_13-00-12_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’il s’agit d’un actif financier entrant dans le champ d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question porte sur une clause contractuelle authentique pouvant modifier le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie en cas d’événement déclencheur.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait n’est donné sur le modèle économique ; l’analyse distingue donc la conclusion SPPI de la catégorie de mesure ultérieure.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut encore satisfaire le critère SPPI si, dans tous les scénarios contractuellement possibles, les flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêts, ou ne diffèrent pas significativement d’un instrument identique sans cette clause. Si ce test échoue, l’actif relève du FVTPL.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause déclenchée ne fait pas perdre le caractère SPPI : IFRS 9 B4.1.10 impose d’examiner les flux avant et après le changement, indépendamment de sa probabilité, et IFRS 9 B4.1.10A permet la qualification SPPI si les flux ne sont pas significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause. Même si ce test SPPI est satisfait, la mesure au coût amorti ne s’applique que si l’actif est détenu pour encaisser les flux contractuels selon IFRS 9 4.1.2.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause de déclenchement ne conduit, dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, qu’à des flux compatibles avec principal et intérêts, ou à des flux non significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est de détenir les actifs pour en percevoir les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut rester éligible à une mesure ultérieure au coût amorti.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la présence d’un déclencheur n’exclut pas en soi SPPI ; le même test contractuel de IFRS 9 B4.1.10 et B4.1.10A s’applique pour vérifier que les flux restent assimilables à principal et intérêts. Si ce test est satisfait, l’option FVOCI de dette n’est ouverte que si le modèle économique consiste à la fois à encaisser les flux contractuels et à vendre les actifs, conformément à IFRS 9 4.1.2A.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause de déclenchement respecte le test SPPI au regard des flux possibles avant et après modification.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’actif est détenu dans un modèle économique dont l’objectif est atteint à la fois par la perception des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs financiers.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI est respecté et que le modèle économique combine encaissement et ventes, l’instrument peut être classé en FVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le FVTPL s’applique si la clause déclenchée fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple si les flux deviennent liés à un facteur incompatible avec un prêt basique ou diffèrent significativement d’un instrument identique sans cette clause selon IFRS 9 B4.1.10A. IFRS 9 4.1.4 prévoit alors la mesure résiduelle par résultat ; les exemples de IFRS 9 B4.1.14 illustrent des clauses non SPPI, telles qu’un index non lié aux risques de prêt basiques.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels possibles en cas de déclenchement ne sont pas exclusivement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts, ou deviennent significativement différents d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI échoue, l’actif ne peut pas être classé au coût amorti ni en FVOCI et doit être mesuré en FVTPL.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are **not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** This list of examples is not exhaustive. InstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety. The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer. InstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates).The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. The interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding. InstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest.The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding. If interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. The fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity. Also, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.) InstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod.The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. **The contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850310811}
