Dans ce cas, peut-il encore répondre au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_13-00-12_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose qu’il s’agit d’un actif financier entrant dans le champ d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la question porte sur l’effet d’une clause contractuelle modifiant le calendrier ou le montant des flux sur le critère SPPI, sans faits supplémentaires sur le modèle économique de gestion.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, une telle clause n’exclut pas automatiquement le critère SPPI. L’actif peut encore être un « prêt basique » si, dans tous les scénarios contractuels pertinents, les flux restent uniquement des remboursements de principal et d’intérêts, sans exposition significative à des risques non basiques (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.10A, B4.1.18).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, le coût amorti reste possible si la clause déclenchée ne fait pas perdre le caractère SPPI des flux et si l’actif est détenu dans un modèle visant à collecter les flux contractuels (IFRS 9 4.1.2, B4.1.10). IFRS 9 exige d’examiner les flux avant et après la modification, indépendamment de la probabilité du déclenchement; si les flux demeurent ceux d’un prêt basique, la classification au coût amorti peut subsister (IFRS 9 B4.1.10, B4.1.11).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est de détenir l’actif pour encaisser les flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"Dans tous les scénarios contractuels possibles, la clause ne crée pas de flux significativement différents de principal et intérêts au sens d’un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause n’introduit pas d’exposition à des variables non basiques, de levier, ou à un rendement lié à des prix d’actions, de matières premières ou à la performance du débiteur.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’instrument peut rester éligible au coût amorti malgré la clause de modification des flux.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows** (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following are examples of contractual terms that result in contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding: **(a)a variable interest rate that consists of consideration for the time value of money, the credit risk associated with the principal amount outstanding during a particular period of time (the consideration for credit risk may be determined at initial recognition only, and so may be fixed) and other basic lending risks and costs, as well as a profit margin; (b)a contractual term that permits the issuer (ie the debtor) to prepay a debt instrument or permits the holder (ie the creditor) to put a debt instrument back to the issuer before maturity and the prepayment amount substantially represents unpaid amounts of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding, which may include reasonable compensation for the early termination of the contract; and (c)a contractual term that permits the issuer or the holder to extend the contractual term of a debt instrument (ie an extension option) and the terms of the extension option result in contractual cash flows during the extension period that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding, which may include reasonable additional compensation for the extension of the contract.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par autres éléments du résultat global\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVOCI reste possible si la clause modifiant les flux respecte le test SPPI et si le modèle économique consiste à la fois à collecter les flux contractuels et à vendre les actifs (IFRS 9 4.1.2A, B4.1.10). Une clause contingente peut encore être compatible avec SPPI si les flux contractuels, dans tous les scénarios, ne deviennent pas significativement différents de ceux d’un prêt basique (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le modèle économique est atteint à la fois par l’encaissement des flux contractuels et par la vente des actifs financiers.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause de déclenchement ne produit pas, dans des scénarios contractuels possibles, des flux significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les flux restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêts sur le principal restant dû.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si le test SPPI est conservé et que le modèle économique est mixte collecte/vente, l’instrument peut être classé en JVOCI.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through other comprehensive income if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows** (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** To make this determination, **the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. **In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. **If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la JVR s’applique si la clause de modification des flux fait échouer le test SPPI, par exemple si elle introduit une exposition à des risques ou indices incompatibles avec un prêt basique, ou des flux significativement différents de principal et intérêts (IFRS 9 B4.1.10A, B4.1.14). IFRS 9 prévoit alors la JVR comme catégorie résiduelle lorsque l’actif n’est ni au coût amorti ni en JVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause de déclenchement fait perdre le caractère SPPI des flux contractuels.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’instrument ne satisfait pas aux conditions de classement au coût amorti ou en JVOCI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si la clause crée une exposition incompatible avec un prêt basique, l’actif doit être classé à la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some cases, a contingent feature gives rise to contractual cash flows that are consistent with a basic lending arrangement both before and after the change in contractual cash flows, but the nature of the contingent event itself does not relate directly to changes in basic lending risks and costs. For example, the interest rate on a loan is adjusted by a specified amount if the debtor achieves a contractually specified reduction in carbon emissions. **In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. **If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following examples illustrate contractual cash flows that are **not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** This list of examples is not exhaustive.\\\\nInstrumentFInstrumentFisabondthatisconvertibleintoafixednumberofequityinstrumentsoftheissuer.The holder would analyse the convertible bond in its entirety.\\\\n**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because they reflect a return that is inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.7A); ie the return is linked to the value of the equity of the issuer.\\\\nInstrumentGInstrumentGisaloanthatpaysaninversefloatinginterestrate(ietheinterestratehasaninverserelationshiptomarketinterestrates).**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nInstrumentHInstrumentHisaperpetualinstrumentbuttheissuermaycalltheinstrumentatanypointandpaytheholdertheparamountplusaccruedinterestdue.InstrumentHpaysamarketinterestratebutpaymentofinterestcannotbemadeunlesstheissuerisabletoremainsolventimmediatelyafterwards.Deferredinterestdoesnotaccrueadditionalinterest.**The contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** That is because the issuer may be required to defer interest payments and additional interest does not accrue on those deferred interest amounts. As a result, interest amounts are not consideration for the time value of money on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nIf interest accrued on the deferred amounts, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.\\\\nThe fact that Instrument H is perpetual does not in itself mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. In effect, a perpetual instrument has continuous (multiple) extension options. Such options may result in contractual cash flows that are payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if interest payments are mandatory and must be paid in perpetuity.\\\\nAlso, the fact that Instrument H is callable does not mean that the contractual cash flows are not payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding unless it is callable at an amount that does not substantially reflect payment of outstanding principal and interest on that principal amount outstanding. Even if the callable amount includes an amount that reasonably compensates the holder for the early termination of the instrument, the contractual cash flows could be payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. (See also paragraph B4.1.12.)\\\\nInstrumentIInstrumentIisaloanwithaninterestratethatisadjustedeveryreportingperiodtotrackthemovementsinamarket-determinedcarbonpriceindexduringtheprecedingreportingperiod.**The contractual cash flows are not solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.**\\\\nThe contractual cash flows are indexed to a variable (the carbon price index), **which is not a basic lending risk or cost. The contractual cash flows are therefore inconsistent with a basic lending arrangement** (see paragraph B4.1.8A).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778850325432}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:55f3757ef2440137105617a37a6438de62a2fd0aa11582cfee8187494c95a943:Un instrument peut comporter des dispositions contractuelles entraînant une modification de l’échéancier ou du montant des flux de trésorerie, par exemple à la survenance d’un événement déclencheur. Dans ce cas, peut-il encore répondre au critère de « prêt basique » (SPPI) ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/50_answer-evals_Q2/runs/2026-05-01_13-00-12_promptfoo-eval-family-q2"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"L’instrument visé est un actif financier entrant dans le champ de la classification d’IFRS 9.\\\",\\n    \\\"La question porte uniquement sur l’effet de clauses contractuelles modifiant le calendrier ou le montant des flux de trésorerie sur le critère SPPI, et non sur l’appréciation détaillée du business model.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, un tel instrument peut encore satisfaire au critère SPPI si, en examinant tous les flux contractuels possibles avant et après le déclenchement, ceux-ci restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt au sens d’un prêt basique. Si la clause introduit une exposition à des risques non basiques ou des flux significativement différents, le critère SPPI échoue.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"amortised_cost\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Coût amorti\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la présence d’un événement déclencheur n’exclut pas automatiquement SPPI: il faut apprécier, pour toute la durée de vie de l’instrument, les flux pouvant naître avant et après la modification (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si ces flux restent des paiements de principal et d’intérêt d’un prêt basique et que l’actif est détenu dans un modèle « hold to collect », la mesure au coût amorti reste possible (IFRS 9 4.1.2, IFRS 9 B4.1.7A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels possibles avant et après le déclenchement restent uniquement des paiements de principal et d’intérêt.\\\",\\n        \\\"La clause ne crée pas d’exposition à des risques ou à une volatilité sans lien avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le business model est de détenir l’actif pour encaisser les flux contractuels.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le coût amorti n’est possible que si la clause contingente ne fait pas échouer le test SPPI et si le modèle économique est « hold to collect ».\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at amortised cost if both of the following conditions are met: **(a)the financial asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a financial asset contains a contractual term that could change the timing or amount of contractual cash flows (for example, if the asset can be prepaid before maturity or its term can be extended), **the entity must determine whether the contractual cash flows that could arise over the life of the instrument due to that contractual term are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding. To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. While the nature of the contingent event in itself is not a determinative factor in assessing whether the contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, it may be an indicator. For example, compare a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if the debtor misses a particular number of payments to a financial instrument with an interest rate that is reset to a higher rate if a specified equity index reaches a particular level. It is more likely in the former case that the contractual cash flows over the life of the instrument will be solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding because of the relationship between missed payments and an increase in credit risk. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. (See also paragraph B4.1.18.)\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In a basic lending arrangement, consideration for the time value of money (see paragraphs B4.1.9A⁠–⁠B4.1.9E) and credit risk are typically the most significant elements of interest. However, in such an arrangement, interest can also include consideration for other basic lending risks (for example, liquidity risk) and costs (for example, administrative costs) associated with holding the financial asset for a particular period of time. In addition, interest can include a profit margin that is consistent with a basic lending arrangement. In extreme economic circumstances, interest can be negative if, for example, the holder of a financial asset either explicitly or implicitly pays for the deposit of its money for a particular period of time (and that fee exceeds the consideration that the holder receives for the time value of money, credit risk and other basic lending risks and costs). **However, contractual terms that introduce exposure to risks or volatility in the contractual cash flows that is unrelated to a basic lending arrangement, such as exposure to changes in equity prices or commodity prices, do not give rise to contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** An originated or a purchased financial asset can be a basic lending arrangement irrespective of whether it is a loan in its legal form.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_other_comprehensive_income\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"JVOCI / juste valeur par OCI\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la clause de modification n’empêche pas à elle seule la qualification SPPI; l’analyse des flux possibles avant et après le changement reste requise (IFRS 9 B4.1.10). Si cette analyse confirme un prêt basique et que le business model consiste à encaisser les flux et à vendre, l’actif peut être classé en FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.2A, IFRS 9 B4.1.10A).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les flux contractuels dans tous les scénarios pertinents demeurent compatibles avec SPPI.\\\",\\n        \\\"Si le déclencheur ne se rattache pas directement aux risques et coûts basiques, les flux avec et sans cette clause ne doivent pas être significativement différents.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le business model est de collecter les flux contractuels et de vendre.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"La FVOCI est possible si la clause contingente conserve SPPI et si le modèle économique est « collect and sell ».\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.2A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair contractual cash flows and selling financial assets and (b)the contractual terms of the financial asset give rise on specified dates to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding.** Paragraphs B4.1.1⁠–⁠B4.1.26 provide guidance on how to apply these conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n To make this determination, the entity must assess the contractual cash flows that could arise both before, and after, the change in contractual cash flows, irrespective of the probability of the change in contractual cash flows occurring.** The entity may also need to assess the nature of any contingent event (ie the trigger) that would change the timing or amount of the contractual cash flows. In the former case, the nature of the contingent event relates directly to, and the contractual cash flows change in the same direction as, changes in basic lending risks and costs. In such a case, when applying paragraph B4.1.10, **the financial asset has contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding if, and only if, in all contractually possible scenarios, the contractual cash flows would not be significantly different from the contractual cash flows on a financial instrument with identical contractual terms, but without such a contingent feature.** In some circumstances, the entity may be able to make that determination by performing a qualitative assessment; but, in other circumstances, it may be necessary to perform a quantitative assessment. If it is clear, with little or no analysis, that the contractual cash flows are not significantly different, an entity need not perform a detailed assessment.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"fair_value_through_profit_or_loss\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Juste valeur par résultat\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la FVPL s’applique si la clause contingente fait échouer SPPI, par exemple parce qu’elle expose l’investisseur à des risques ou à une volatilité étrangers à un prêt basique, ou si les flux deviennent significativement différents (IFRS 9 B4.1.7A, IFRS 9 B4.1.10A). IFRS 9 prévoit alors la FVPL par défaut lorsqu’un actif ne relève ni du coût amorti ni de la FVOCI (IFRS 9 4.1.4).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La clause introduit des flux non compatibles avec un prêt basique.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien les flux contractuels possibles avec la clause sont significativement différents de ceux d’un instrument identique sans cette clause.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien l’actif ne relève pas d’un des deux business models ouvrant droit aux catégories fondées sur SPPI.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si la clause fait échouer SPPI, la classification bascule vers la juste valeur par résultat.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"4.1.4\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A financial asset shall be measured at fair value through profit or loss **unless it is measured at amortised cost in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2 or at fair value through other comprehensive income in accordance with paragraph 4.1.2A.** However an entity may make an irrevocable election at initial recognition for particular investments in equity instruments that would otherwise be measured at fair value through profit or loss to present subsequent changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (see paragraphs 5.7.5⁠–⁠5.7.6). E13, E14\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs9\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B4.1.7A\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Contractual cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding are consistent with a basic lending arrangement.
